item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I am aware that Stonefish is one of the site's more divisive writers but this is probably their best piece (though I like most of them).

I like that it's short, and that it's willfully just completely outside general Foundation frame of reference. Whatever is happening here they're still clearly trying to maintain their usual tone and standard of operation, but it's not really working.

For the record, for the people who are confused, Stonefish has actually explained the inspiration behind this article before. Whether you find that explanation satisfactory or pretentious is going to rest on your personal taste as far as this sort of thing goes. Though I think the phrase "murder scene elemental" would work just as well for a certain sort of person (namely: me, given a lifelong habit of conceptualizing things as D&D monsters).

Personally I think this is one of the best shorter Series 5 articles that exist right now and I'd hate to see it removed.

easy and enthusiastic +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Which part?
Molecules stop moving? Everything on the planet is constantly vibrating on at least an atomic level because temperature is just the speed of vibration, so that's a very unusual thing to happen. Unless you're maybe at absolute zero, which there's no indication is happening here, (plus the very next statement being about cinematic aspect ratios) it implies that part of the world is temporarily being turned into a photograph in a sense.

Cinematic aspect ratios are like… Ok you know when we switched to wide screen? Stuff used to be filmed in one aspect ratio and now it's filmed in another. You take a photo and Instagram chops off the top and bottom, or the sides. That's a different aspect ratio now than it was when you took it. It's a size, in essence, but a two dimensional ratio of size, so that what they film fits on every current model tv and not just one size of tv.

There's definitely other things I just did not follow, but that one is basic filmmaking, which I have dabbled in, so hopefully I cleared up that sentence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it it’s good 1+

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's pretty much exactly my feeling. I had it explained to me and it makes sense now, but it's not an explanation that anyone could really deduce from the information given, nor does it explain every aspect of the article - only like 70% of it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I get that there's an underlying logic to all this, but realistically speaking, this is still a pile of hot nonsense. It's enjoyable nonsense, though! Especially the temporal/causal fuckery aspect. "Age of corpse is several years before individual's birth" is an absolutely killer line. If this piece doesn't end up surviving, that bit needs to be used somewhere.

item.title: MOD POST - INFO - OPEN
item.content:encoded:
While not explicitely rulebreaking, the tone of your crit comes off as super shitty.

If you've got an issue with something "ugh" is not really a valuable, or good critique.

Keep this in mind going forward.

Three times a month, SCP-4302 has manifested.

ugh

+ show block
– hide block

in the development

in development

on the molecular level

Absolute 0?

Following this, [DATA EXPUNGED] this marks the emergence of [REDACTED]

About when I started being bored

Once it locks on to the 'target' [REDACTED] before the symptoms progress, typically mimicking dementia, but not dementia itself.

From the little I understand due to your overexpungement it appears people get dementia that the Foundation magically figures out is not dementia?

Age of corpse is several years before individual's birth.

Again, overexpungement doesn't make your article more interesting

There is no memetic effect to SCP-4302. However, the sky above the event displays no stars

Read understanding memetics

Forensic paleontologists working concurrently in seventy-five regions have noted the evidence of a murder stretches as far back as the Cretaceous Period.

[[collapsible show="I don't care if it's an insult to your intelligence" hide="there were no humans in th Cretaceous Period"]][[/collapsible]]

The artifacts recovered from Mongolia and the Hell Creek Badlands indicate there never was any blood on the lens.

How is that relevant to anything? Really, this feels like the story's been missing a piece. Oh right, it is missing a piece.

Also there's far too much exposition in the SCP section


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

EDIT: Read your comment, the article must stand on its own. An article that's intentionally underexplained and sloppily written is still underexplained and sloppily written. Also they could just, you know, copypaste your comment as it didn't cause any revulsion in me?

Overall sounds like a lot of stuff that isn't properly explained in the article and barely connected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Next, patches of space across the technology park cease movement on the molecular level, with dimensions matching those of various cinematic aspect ratios.

I have no idea what this means and it is giving me a hard time trying to visualize it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It did, slightly. I feel like there's multiple levels of abstraction here that detract from the piece's impact. Some elements seem to come out of nowhere while not really adding to the piece, like the bit about Mongolia. Maybe I have some bias toward rigid narrative structures (even if obfuscated) that prevents me from truly "getting" the piece.

I did like the circular aspect of some segments, however. Like:

Testing is to be done with remotely activated drones, who are then to be constructed in order to facilitate testing.

That one really clicked for me after your explanation and re-reading the piece.

I've read your other pieces and enjoyed most of them. However, I'll refrain from rating this one.

Thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Everything in the article is intentional.

To put in more clearly without spoiling what is specifically going on here:

+ Title for showing text
- Title for hiding text

there’s a parking lot where time is messed up, and it’s implied that there is a hostile force that is a) intangible and b) not strictly alive. Whatever it is, it creates a thing that is a mimic of a chosen target, and begins to consume the target’s history, ending with their birth, ending up with the oddly aged corpses ending up in the wombs of nearby mothers. It is connected to, or caused by a murder on this site involving a camera that was so vile the universe revolted, and there’s something about that murder, and this phenomenon that is so horrible that the Foundation has expunged the important details.

Another idea was for this to be an inverse SCP-087. Both are unknown predatory forces that have colonized and distorted a space in order to hunt, with an accompanying humanoid puppet, but many of the details are flipped. Time is distorted, not space. Image is important, not sound. It involves a university student, not a university building. There is something so horrible at the bottom that you, the reader will never know what it is, but it’s in the key details and not an exploration log. Like SCP-087, you’re supposed to wonder how the details fit together. Why do the stars matter to memetics? Why the time distortion? Why is there a lens appearing in the fossil record? Why even is it Keter? What could be so scary about the duplicate in the image? Nothing is scarier than what your own mind imagines. I was trying to bridge the gap between Series I and Series V here.

Does this help?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm terribly confused by this article. There seems to be several instance of non-sequitur throughout the piece.

For instance:

There is no memetic effect to SCP-4302. However, the sky above the event displays no stars.

I don't see how memetic effects are related to the absence of stars?

Or:

If in the event of personnel becoming a target, the Foundation as a whole should be prepared to have records changed or deleted. This is normal. Agents assigned are to investigate maternity wards worldwide for any personnel lost to SCP-4302.

I really don't see how investigating maternity wards has anything to do with the previous sentences.

Overall I feel like there's a lack of thought continuity between sentences and paragraphs. There's an interesting story here but it reads like someone constantly jumping from a subject to another, never quite finishing their thoughts.

This may sound harsh, but I have no idea how to convey the following question otherwise: You mention that targets affected by the anomaly begin showing signs of dementia. Are you trying to imply that whoever is writing the article is a target? The writing feels like it's clunky and strange enough to imply that but I can't find anything that confirms my suspicion. I ask because I've read and enjoyed a few pieces that did delve into incoherent writing as a deliberate stylistic choice.

If you can confirm that it's intentional and maybe make that slightly clearer in the article itself I'd happily rate this up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could anyone explain this for me?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After edits, the numbers 35 and 70 appear again in reference to lens sizes (in millimeters) and 35 appears again in kilometers. Ultimately while the numbers appear, the dimensions that dictate containment do not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The fact the dimensions of 35x70 never appear again in the article and are never mentioned again make them feel like arbitrary values.

Are you sure they aren't mentioned later?

E: I just realized that the author put a new footnote after your comment. oopsie.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a bit of a weird one, really leaning into redactions and hints while leaving what is going on very unclear. I'm always in favor of experimental stuff, so I've given an upvote, but I do think it could be a bit cleaner than it currently is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Clinical tone here is really off to me and this just seems like it lacks some polish.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That is correct, the fault was in my wording of "on the full moon" as opposed to "during the full moon"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Setting it to 200px seemed to fix it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The "full moon" is not an instant of time, it is a period of time. "Three times a month during the full moon" isn't a contradictory statement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am aware of that, but the choice of the number is deliberate

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As Veiedhimaedhr pointed out there are multiple inconsistency in this article but what sealed the final nail on the coffin was saying three times a month every full moon. They only occur twice a month. And not referring to the SCP as one name but switching it through out the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh I love this. I don't think anyone gets it because it's very, very diffuse and obscure… Although it's said right at the beggining.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This can be better.

The container for SCP-4302 is located within Site-812. It is to be constructed out of plywood and polyurethane foam, and must measure exactly 35 meters by 70 meters.

The fact the dimensions of 35x70 never appear again in the article and are never mentioned again make them feel like arbitrary values. You also refer to SCP-4302 as both a phenomenon and a ‘thing,’ further confusing the article.

Three times a month, SCP-4302 has manifested.
SCP-4302 occurs three times a month, every month, on the full moon,

The same information is repeated twice, and the second is not only clinically informal, but also inaccurate: full moons occur twice a month

There are pieces which are confusing, vague and ‘deep’ while still maintaining the character of and SCP article. I do not believe this does so. I do believe this is permanently the case, however; I believe that this can be as deep as desired without sacrificing the feeling this was written by the Foundation.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hmm. Setting width to 300px didn't fix the appearance of the second image.

That aside, I like how this uses the old Series 1 style without excessively redacting detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This feels extremely choppy to me. The redactions just destroy the flow… unless I'm missing something huge… but I have to vote with what I see, and this gets a -1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't seem to make it any smaller, though I'd like to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really don't like how big the last image is, it kinda breaks it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: photographs by me.
